name: Croagh Patrick
longDescription:
County Mayo, Ireland Croagh Patrick (Irish: Cruach Phadraig, "Patrick's Stack") is Ireland's holiest mountain, a 764-meter
                (2,507-foot) peak overlooking Clew Bay in County Mayo. For over 5,000 years, this conical quartzite
                mountain has been a place of pilgrimage - first for pre-Christian Celtic peoples who honored the
                harvest god Crom Dubh, and then for Christians following in the footsteps of St. Patrick. According
                to tradition, in 441 CE, Patrick fasted on the summit for forty days and forty nights, emulating
                Moses and Christ, and it was here that he banished all snakes from Ireland. Today, over 100,000
                pilgrims climb "the Reek" annually, many barefoot, making it one of the most important Christian
                pilgrimage sites in Europe.

geography: Elevated terrain with commanding views; often snow-capped or shrouded in mist, symbolizing proximity to the divine

keyMyths.title: Saint Patrick's Fast and the Banishment of Snakes
keyMyths.description: Croagh Patrick is most famously associated with Saint Patrick's 40-day fast atop the mountain in 441 AD. During this period, he is said to have banished all snakes from Ireland into the sea, symbolizing the triumph of Christianity over paganism.

keyMyths.title: The Burning of the Demons
keyMyths.description: While fasting, Saint Patrick was tormented by demons. In a climactic act, he rang a bell and cast them into a pit, or into the sea, thus cleansing Ireland and preparing it for the acceptance of Christianity.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Lóchru's Grave
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Saint Patrick's charioteer, Lóchru, died on Croagh Patrick. His grave is said to be marked by a cairn of stones near the summit, adding to the mountain's sacred associations.

keyMyths.title: Pre-Christian Pilgrimage Site
keyMyths.description: Archaeological evidence and folklore suggest that Croagh Patrick was a significant pilgrimage site long before the arrival of Christianity. Its prominence may have been linked to the festival of Lughnasa, marking the beginning of the harvest season.

keyMyths.title: Connection to Crom Dubh
keyMyths.description: Some scholars believe that Croagh Patrick was originally associated with Crom Dubh, a pre-Christian deity linked to agriculture and fertility. The mountain may have been a site of ritual sacrifice or offerings to ensure a bountiful harvest before its Christianization.
keyMyths.source: Peter Berresford Ellis, *A Dictionary of Irish Mythology* (1987)

sources.name: Tírechán, *Collectanea de S. Patricio*
sources.description: A 7th-century collection of material relating to Saint Patrick, offering some of the earliest accounts of his life and deeds.
sources.title: Tírechán, *Collectanea de S. Patricio*

sources.name: Vita Tripartita Sancti Patricii
sources.description: An 11th-century Irish text providing a comprehensive, though often legendary, account of Saint Patrick's life in three parts.
sources.title: Vita Tripartita Sancti Patricii

sources.name: John O'Donovan, *Ordnance Survey Letters*
sources.description: Letters written by John O'Donovan during the Ordnance Survey of Ireland in the 19th century, containing valuable insights into local folklore and traditions.
sources.title: John O'Donovan, *Ordnance Survey Letters*

sources.name: Máire MacNeill, *The Festival of Lughnasa*
sources.description: A detailed study of the Lughnasa festival, exploring its origins, rituals, and connections to sites like Croagh Patrick.
sources.title: Máire MacNeill, *The Festival of Lughnasa*

sources.name: Peter Berresford Ellis, *A Dictionary of Irish Mythology*
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary of Irish mythology, providing information on deities, figures, and sacred sites.
sources.title: Peter Berresford Ellis, *A Dictionary of Irish Mythology*
